us-politics.headline: US Warplanes Shot Down Over Iran, Crew Member Missing
us-politics.body:
Two U.S. fighter jets and two helicopters were hit by Iranian fire during ongoing military operations, with at least one crew member still missing. Recovery efforts remain active. The incidents mark a serious escalation in what has become an active U.S. military engagement with Iran.

On Capitol Hill, Republican Senator John Curtis called for ending operations without a formal declaration of war, and Rep. Nancy Mace voiced concern about further escalation. NPR notes Trump's wartime rhetoric departs sharply from the measured language used by past presidents, a contrast that critics argue raises the stakes diplomatically. A Democrat also condemned prediction market Polymarket for taking bets on the missing pilot's fate.

sources.title: What to know about US fighter jets hit in Iran war
2 US helicopters hit during recovery efforts in Iran
GOP senator calls to end Iran operations without formal declaration of war
Two U.S. warplanes shot down; search ongoing in Iran for 1 missing crew member
Mace ‘concerned about escalation’ in Iran war
Democrat rips Polymarket for taking bets on fate of US pilot shot down over Iran
How Trump's wartime rhetoric differs from past presidents and why that matters

us-politics.headline: Trump Cabinet Shifts, DOJ Leadership, and Budget Battles
us-politics.body:
Trump is weighing further Cabinet changes but wants to avoid a broad shake-up, according to the Washington Post. Todd Blanche has taken over as acting Attorney General following Pam Bondi's departure, inheriting both the DOJ's caseload and Trump's expectations. Blanche told reporters there is no evidence in the Epstein files that Epstein worked as a spy.

On the budget, Trump proposed a $10 billion fund for D.C. construction and a defense spending plan that drew pushback from Republican Senator Susan Collins, who criticized domestic funding cuts. GOP senators also praised Army Chief of Staff General George after his ouster by Defense Secretary Hegseth, signaling quiet friction within the party over military leadership decisions.

sources.title: Trump weighs more Cabinet changes, but wants to avoid ‘massive shake-up’
Todd Blanche takes on DOJ’s top job and the burden of Trump’s expectations
What to know about Trump’s defense budget proposal
Blanche: No evidence in Epstein files he was a spy
Trump proposes $10B fund for D.C. construction, beautification projects
GOP praise pours in for Army chief of staff ousted by Hegseth
Susan Collins knocks funding cuts in White House budget proposal

us-politics.headline: States Sue Over Mail-In Voting Order; Colorado Lawyers Resist ICE Pledge
us-politics.body:
Oregon and a coalition of Democratic-led states filed lawsuits challenging Trump's executive order restricting vote-by-mail, with Oregon's attorney general calling it a source of "confusion, chaos and distrust." The legal fights center on whether the federal government can override state-run election systems. Courts have not yet ruled.

Separately, Colorado lawyers report that the state court's electronic filing system now requires them to certify they will not assist ICE - a requirement Fox News highlighted as a flashpoint between state legal institutions and federal immigration enforcement. The two disputes together reflect a widening conflict between state governments and the Trump administration across immigration and election policy.

sources.title: ‘Confusion, chaos and distrust’: Oregon challenges Trump’s order restricting vote-by-mail
Colorado lawyers say court e-file system now makes them certify they won’t assist ICE
Democratic-led states sue over Trump mail-in ballot order

us-politics.headline: Trump Signs College Sports Order; Religion Message on Good Friday
us-politics.body:
Trump signed an executive order targeting college sports reform, his second such directive aimed at reshaping NCAA governance. The order focuses on athlete compensation and competitive fairness, though critics note executive power over collegiate athletics has unclear legal grounding. The Washington Post reports the move follows an earlier order that produced limited results.

On Good Friday, Trump issued a message declaring "America needs God" and pointing to what he called a "resurgence of religion" in the country. Fox News covered the statement favorably, framing it as consistent with his broader appeal to religious conservatives. Separately, retirement savings rates are declining according to a new payroll firm report, adding an economic undercurrent to a politically turbulent week.

sources.title: Trump signs order aimed at reforming college sports
Trump says ‘America needs God’ in Good Friday message touting ‘resurgence of religion'
Trump again orders changes to ‘save’ college sports
Warning sign? Retirement saving habits eroding, payroll firm says

au-politics.headline: Drug bust, murder charge, missing woman: crime dominates Easter weekend
au-politics.body:
Nine people face charges after police uncovered an alleged plot to import tonnes of cocaine and methamphetamine into Australian waters via a so-called mother ship. The operation came to light after a trawler sank, alerting authorities to the syndicate. Separately, a woman has been charged with murder following the death of a 24-year-old in Laverton, and two people were arrested in connection with the investigation into the movements of missing man Dezi Freeman near Porepunkah in Victoria.

In Western Australia, police are appealing for public help to locate a woman missing in Albany's national park near The Gap. The cluster of serious incidents across multiple states underscores a busy Easter period for law enforcement.

sources.title: Police ask for help in locating missing woman in Albany national park
Victoria police arrest two people as part of Dezi Freeman investigation
Two arrested as part of investigation into movements of Dezi Freeman
Woman charged with murder following investigation into death of 24yo
Nine charged over alleged conspiracy to import tonnes of cocaine and meth via ‘mother ship’ in Australian waters
Sunken trawler alerts police to alleged drug-smuggling plan

au-politics.headline: Victorian election heats up as challenger targets Premier Allan's seat
au-politics.body:
Andrew Lethlean has announced he will contest Jacinda Allan's seat of Bendigo East at the next Victorian election, setting up a direct challenge to the sitting premier. The move signals growing opposition confidence in a seat that Labor has held but which could face pressure amid broader questions about the Allan government's performance.

The Spectator Australia has run commentary this week criticising what it calls 'parasite politics' in Western Australia, reflecting ongoing right-of-centre frustration with Labor administrations at the state level. The contest in Bendigo East will be closely watched as a bellwether for Labor's vulnerability heading into the election cycle.

sources.title: Lethlean to challenge Victorian premier in Bendigo East
The West’s parasite politics

au-politics.headline: Fuel supply stable but panic buying persists, farmers demand fairer prices
au-politics.body:
Australia's energy minister says fuel supplies remain strong and fewer service stations are running dry, but high demand driven by consumer anxiety is persisting at the bowser. The disconnect between actual supply and public behaviour is complicating the picture for retailers and distributors.

Farmers are using the moment to press supermarkets for higher prices on fresh produce, arguing that elevated fuel and transport costs are squeezing margins to breaking point. The dual pressure - on consumers at the pump and on growers in the supply chain - points to broader cost-of-living tensions that are likely to feature in upcoming political debate.

sources.title: Fewer service stations running out of petrol and diesel as Australia’s fuel supplies remain strong, energy minister says
Fuel demand stays high, as farmers urge supermarkets to pay more for fresh produce

au-politics.headline: Sydney data centres spark blackout and housing fears among local councils
au-politics.body:
Sydney councils are raising alarms over a wave of proposed data centres, warning they could strain the electricity grid to the point of causing blackouts, consume land earmarked for housing, and pose health risks to nearby residents. The concerns put local government on a collision course with the tech industry and state planning authorities.

Critics argue the centres draw enormous power loads while delivering few local jobs or community benefits. Supporters say digital infrastructure is essential for economic growth. The dispute reflects a wider tension in Australian cities between the demands of a growing digital economy and the needs of communities competing for space and reliable services.

sources.title: Sydney councils fear new datacentres could cause blackouts, block housing and affect locals’ health

world.headline: US-Israel War on Iran Escalates as Missiles Hit Both Sides
world.body:
Now in its 36th day, the US-Israeli military campaign against Iran is widening fast. Tehran downed two American warplanes and launched missile strikes on residential areas in central Israel, while US-Israeli forces bombed bridges in Lebanon and continued striking Iranian infrastructure. At least one person was killed at a UAE gas facility after a partially intercepted attack, signaling the conflict is pulling in Gulf neighbors.

The humanitarian toll inside Iran is mounting. The WHO reports more than 20 attacks on Iranian healthcare facilities since March 1. Survivors of a bridge strike described scenes of chaos and terror. The Strait of Hormuz remains a flashpoint, with serious debate now underway over whether force will be used to reopen the critical shipping lane.

sources.title: Iran war: What is happening on day 36 of US-Israeli attacks?
Iranian missile attack hits residential areas in central Israel
Rescue team in Iran face 'harrowing and dangerous' search for US crew member

Will force be used to reopen Strait of Hormuz?
Survivors of Iran bridge attack recall terror of US-Israeli strike

At least one killed at UAE’s Habshan gas facility after intercepted attack

world.headline: Artemis II Crew Reaches Halfway Point, Sends Back Stunning Earth Images
world.body:
NASA's Artemis II crew has passed the halfway mark of their lunar flyby mission, releasing the first photographs of Earth taken from deep space since the Apollo era. The images, described as spectacular, show a vivid full Earth against the black of space and have drawn widespread attention.

The mission marks a major milestone in NASA's push to return humans to the vicinity of the Moon. A separate search-and-rescue operation in Iran is underway for a US crew member, adding an unexpected and dangerous dimension to the mission's human story.

sources.title: Artemis II crew now halfway to moon as they take 'spectacular' image of Earth
NASA unveils first images of Earth from Artemis II
Rescue team in Iran face 'harrowing and dangerous' search for US crew member
NASA releases stunning first photos of Earth from Artemis II moon mission

world.headline: Cuba Frees 2,000 Prisoners as US Pressure Bites
world.body:
Cuba has begun releasing more than 2,000 prisoners in what analysts are calling one of the most significant concessions by Havana in years. The move comes directly amid sustained pressure from Washington, suggesting the Trump administration's coercive approach is producing results, at least on this front.

The releases are expected to continue in phases. Critics caution that political prisoners may not be among those freed, and that the gesture could be tactical rather than a genuine shift in Cuban governance.

sources.title: Cuba begins releasing more than 2,000 prisoners as US pressure mounts
Cuba releases over 2,000 prisoners amid mounting US pressure

world.headline: Democrat States Sue Trump Over Ballot Rules; Powell Subpoenas Tossed
world.body:
Two dozen Democrat-led states filed suit against the Trump administration over new restrictions on mail-in voting, arguing the limits amount to voter suppression ahead of future elections. The legal challenge is one of the broadest coordinated state-level actions against federal election policy in recent memory.

Separately, a federal judge upheld the dismissal of subpoenas targeting Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, blocking what critics called an attempt to politically pressure the central bank. Both cases reflect deepening legal battles over the boundaries of executive power in Washington.

sources.title: Two dozen Democrat-led states sue Trump over mail-in ballot limits
US judge upholds decision to toss subpoenas into Fed Chair Jerome Powell

ai.headline: Anthropic's Busy Week: Biotech Deal, PAC, and Claude Controversy
ai.body:
Anthropic is moving fast on multiple fronts. The company reportedly acquired biotech startup Coefficient Bio for $400 million, launched a political action committee to expand its Washington influence, and is drawing strong interest in private markets - though SpaceX's anticipated IPO may soon compete for the same investor dollars.

On the product side, Anthropic drew backlash by blocking Claude Code subscribers from using OpenClaw without paying extra. Users and developers called it a de facto ban. OpenClaw itself compounded the drama by surfacing fresh security concerns, with researchers advising users to assume their accounts may have been compromised.

sources.title: Anthropic is having a moment in the private markets; SpaceX could spoil the party
Anthropic essentially bans OpenClaw from Claude by making subscribers pay extra
Tell HN: Anthropic no longer allowing Claude Code subscriptions to use OpenClaw
OpenClaw gives users yet another reason to be freaked out about security
Anthropic buys biotech startup Coefficient Bio in $400M deal: Reports
Anthropic ramps up its political activities with a new PAC

ai.headline: OpenAI Reshuffles Leadership as Two Executives Step Back
ai.body:
OpenAI is reorganizing at the top. COO Brad Lightcap moves into a new "special projects" role, while Fidji Simo is taking medical leave. The company's head of AGI is also stepping away temporarily. Three senior departures or role changes in quick succession raises questions about internal stability at a critical moment for the company.

The shuffles come as OpenAI faces intensifying competition and scrutiny over its direction. No successors or interim replacements have been named publicly.

sources.title: OpenAI executive shuffle includes new role for COO Brad Lightcap to lead ‘special projects’
OpenAI’s AGI boss is taking a leave of absence
OpenAI’s Fidji Simo Is Taking Medical Leave Amid an Executive Shake-Up

ai.headline: AI's Power Hunger Meets Political Headwinds and Public Backlash
ai.body:
AI companies are building private natural gas plants to feed data centers that the grid cannot support - a bet on fossil fuels that carries both environmental and regulatory risk. Trump's push for a domestic AI data center buildout is meanwhile stalling, undercut by his own tariffs raising hardware costs.

Public appetite for the infrastructure is thin. Surveys show people prefer Amazon warehouses to data centers as neighbors. A separate study found AI users are increasingly surrendering independent reasoning to LLMs - a trend researchers are calling "cognitive surrender."

sources.title: "Cognitive surrender" leads AI users to abandon logical thinking, research finds
Trump ignores biggest reasons his AI data center buildout is failing
AI companies are building huge natural gas plants to power data centers. What could go wrong?
People would rather have an Amazon warehouse in their backyard than a data center

ai.headline: Artemis II Heads to the Moon as NASA Budget Faces Cuts
ai.body:
The Artemis II crew is en route to the Moon and by all accounts the mission is running smoothly. The astronauts have already captured striking images of Earth from deep space. It is the first crewed lunar mission in over fifty years.

The timing is awkward for NASA. Trump has proposed steep budget cuts to the agency even as the mission unfolds, putting future Artemis flights and broader deep-space ambitions in doubt. Separately, Elon Musk is reportedly requiring banks working on a SpaceX IPO to purchase Grok subscriptions - an unusual demand that blurs the line between business and leverage.

sources.title: Trump proposes steep cut to NASA budget as astronauts head for the Moon
As Artemis II zooms to the Moon, everything seems to be going swimmingly
Elon Musk insists banks working on SpaceX IPO must buy Grok subscriptions
Artemis II crew take “spectacular” image of Earth

btc.headline: Schwab Enters Spot Crypto Trading as ETF Optimism Grows
btc.body:
Charles Schwab plans to launch direct spot Bitcoin and Ether trading for retail customers in the first half of 2026, a major signal that traditional brokerages are done sitting out the crypto market. The move follows sustained institutional pressure and growing client demand.

Analyst James Seyffart argues Bitcoin ETFs will ultimately surpass gold ETFs in size, citing Bitcoin's broader and more flexible use cases. Together, these developments suggest the infrastructure pulling mainstream capital into crypto is maturing fast. The question is no longer whether Wall Street participates - it is how quickly.

sources.title: Bitcoin ETFs 'will be larger' than gold ETFs: Analyst
Schwab plans spot bitcoin, ether trading launch in first half of 2026

btc.headline: Circle Faces Backlash After $285M Hack Goes Unfrozen
btc.body:
Circle is under sharp criticism after failing to freeze USDC funds stolen in a $285 million hack of the Drift protocol. Critics argue the stablecoin issuer had both the technical ability and the responsibility to act, and its inaction raises hard questions about what USDC's freeze mechanism is actually for.

The episode exposes a core tension in stablecoin design - centralized controls exist, but their use remains discretionary. For DeFi protocols that rely on USDC as a base layer, that ambiguity is now a documented risk.

sources.title: Circle under fire after $285 million Drift hack over inaction to freeze stolen USDC

btc.headline: Quantum Threat to Bitcoin Is Real but Distant
btc.body:
Headlines claiming quantum computers can crack Bitcoin in nine minutes are misleading. The research describes attacks on isolated cryptographic primitives under idealized conditions, not a live assault on the Bitcoin network, which layers multiple defenses and requires breaking far more than a single key.

That said, the underlying progress is real and the timeline for quantum-resistant cryptography matters. Bitcoin developers have time, but not unlimited time, to migrate the network to post-quantum standards before the threat becomes practical.

sources.title: Here's what 'cracking' bitcoin in 9 minutes by quantum computers actually means

btc.headline: Ethereum Foundation Stakes ETH; Cambodia Targets Crypto Scammers
btc.body:
The Ethereum Foundation is close to hitting its 70,000 staked ETH target, a move that signals stronger institutional commitment to the network's proof-of-stake security and aligns the Foundation more directly with validator incentives.

Separately, Cambodian lawmakers are pushing legislation that would impose severe prison sentences on crypto scammers - part of a broader regional effort to crack down on fraud operations that have flourished across Southeast Asia. Both developments reflect a maturing ecosystem where foundations take on more active roles and governments move from warnings to hard enforcement.

sources.title: Ethereum Foundation nearly reaches 70,000 staked ETH goal
Cambodian lawmakers propose severe prison time for crypto scammers

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leek and Mushroom Galette
food.description: A free-form pastry tart with caramelised leeks and shiitake mushrooms lacquered in white miso butter
food.tip: Brush the miso glaze on in the last 8 minutes of baking only — earlier and it scorches before the pastry is done

exercise.name: Loaded Carry Circuit
exercise.equipment: Two kettlebells or heavy shopping bags, a clear 10-metre path
exercise.description: Cycle through farmer's carries, suitcase carries, and overhead carries with short rest intervals — brutally effective for grip strength, core stability, and real-world fitness

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity Over Time
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains fundamentally the same object — philosophers have extended this to human cells, memories, and selfhood
learn.why: On a quiet Saturday it's a genuinely unsettling rabbit hole: neuroscientist Derek Parfit's modern take in 'Reasons and Persons' argues personal identity is less fixed than we assume, which has surprising implications for how you think about regret, ambition, and who you owe loyalty to
